mnemonics.title: UTAW - Scheduling Criteria Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: CPU UTILIZATION, THROUGHPUT, AVERAGE WAITING TIME, TURNOVER TIME, RESPONSE TIME - Remember as UTAT (You Take A Test): Utilization, Throughput, Average waiting time, Turnaround time, Response time.

mnemonics.title: TWT Formula Helper
mnemonics.content: Turnaround Time = Waiting Time + Burst Time. Think T = W + B (T stands for Total = Waiting + Burst).

mnemonics.title: System Type Matcher
mnemonics.content: BATCH = Throughput, INTERACTIVE = Response Time, REAL-TIME = Deadlines. Use B-I-R = BIB (Batch-In-Rush): Batch needs Throughput, Interactive needs Response.

acronyms.expansion: Central Processing Unit - The main processor that executes processes
acronyms.usage: CPU Utilization measures how busy the processor is

acronyms.expansion: First Come First Serve - A non-preemptive scheduling algorithm
acronyms.usage: FCFS tends to have high average waiting time and convoy effect

acronyms.expansion: Shortest Job First - Schedules shortest burst time process first
acronyms.usage: SJF provides minimum average waiting time but may cause starvation

acronyms.expansion: Round Robin - Time slice based preemptive scheduling
acronyms.usage: RR ensures fair CPU sharing but may increase average turnaround time

acronyms.expansion: Waiting Time - Total time process spends in ready queue
acronyms.usage: Minimizing WT improves overall system performance

acronyms.expansion: Arrival Time - Time when process enters ready queue
acronyms.usage: AT affects scheduling order and waiting time calculation

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a timeline: ARRIVAL at left, COMPLETION at right. The space between is turnaround time, composed of waiting (shaded gray) and burst (solid color) sections.
Draw the ready queue as a horizontal line with processes as blocks. The waiting time is the total length each block sits before reaching the CPU (front of queue).
For interactive systems, response time is like a lightning bolt - from finger press (request) to screen change (first output) - it must be instant.
Think of a car speedometer 0-100%. CPU utilization at 80% means the needle is at 80, showing how much of the time the engine (CPU) is running vs idling.
Imagine bars of different heights representing completed processes per hour. Higher bars = higher throughput = more work done in same time.

analogies.analogy: Like a restaurant kitchen - CPU utilization is the percentage of time the chef is cooking vs standing idle waiting for orders.
Like a factory assembly line - throughput is how many complete products roll off the line per hour.
Like a dry cleaning service - turnaround time is from when you drop off clothes to when they're ready for pickup.
Like people in a doctor's waiting room - waiting time is total time spent sitting before seeing the doctor.
Like a live chat support - response time is how quickly the support agent types back after you send your message.
Like taking turns on a swing set - fairness means everyone gets equal time to swing, no one pushes others out permanently.
Like a team project where one person does all the writing while another does nothing - balancing means distributing work so all team members stay busy.
